In C++, the starter code is a function inside the Solution class. The code would be:

int countKDifference(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
    int count = 0;
    int n = nums.size();
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) {
            if (abs(nums[i] - nums[j]) == k) {
                ++count;
            }
        }
    }
    return count;
}

class Solution {
public:
    int countKDifference(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        int count = 0;
        int n = nums.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) {
                if (abs(nums[i] - nums[j]) == k) {
                    ++count;
                }
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int countKDifference(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        int count = 0;
        int n = nums.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) {
                if (abs(nums[i] - nums[j]) == k) {
                    ++count;
                }
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
};
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(n^2)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The brute-force approach checks all pairs (i, j) with i < j and counts those with absolute difference equal to k. It is efficient enough for the problem's constraints (n ≤ 200). Strengths: simplicity and clarity. Weaknesses: O(n²) time complexity may be suboptimal for larger inputs.
